keywords: drywall screws, drywall screw types, drywall screw sizes, construction fasteners, drywall installation

heading.en: Market Overview: Why Drywall Screw Selection Matters

body.en: The global drywall screws market was valued at approximately USD 2.58 billion in 2024 and is projected to reach USD 4.24 billion by 2033, growing at a CAGR of 5.7%. This growth is driven by increased residential construction and renovation projects, growing adoption of metal stud framing, demand for corrosion-resistant fasteners in damp environments, and rise of modular and prefabricated construction. For procurement teams, understanding drywall screw specifications is increasingly critical for project success.

Drywall screws are specialized fasteners designed for attaching gypsum board (drywall/sheetrock) to wood or metal framing. Unlike traditional nails, drywall screws offer superior holding power — threads bite into framing material, reduced nail pops — secure fastening prevents backing out, faster installation — compatible with power tools and screw guns, and better finish quality — bugle head design prevents paper tearing.

heading.en: Drywall Screw Types: Head Styles Explained

body.en: The head type determines how the screw sits in the drywall. Bugle head is the most common — sits flush without tearing paper, ideal for standard drywall-to-wood applications. Flat head provides a larger bearing surface for metal stud applications. Modified bugle head combines bugle shape with trimming ribs for clean countersinking. Pan head sits on the surface, used for specialty applications requiring a wider bearing surface.

heading.en: Thread Types: Fine Thread vs Coarse Thread

body.en: Choosing the right thread type is critical. Fine thread (S-type) has more threads per inch, designed for metal studs, self-tapping into thin-gauge steel. Coarse thread (W-type) has wider thread spacing, designed for wood studs, bites aggressively into wood fibers. Combination thread features both fine and coarse sections for versatile substrate compatibility.

Drywall screw sizes are designated by gauge (diameter) and length. Common sizes range from #6 to #10 gauge and 1 to 3 inches in length. The most frequently used sizes are #6×1-1/4 for single-layer drywall on wood studs, #6×1-5/8 for double-layer drywall on wood studs, and #6×1 for single-layer drywall on metal studs.

Coating selection affects both performance and cost. Black phosphate is the most economical option for dry indoor environments. Zinc plating provides moderate corrosion resistance for damp areas. Ceramic coating offers superior corrosion resistance for outdoor and high-humidity applications. Stainless steel provides maximum corrosion resistance for marine and chemical environments but at a premium cost.

heading.en: Selection Guide: How to Choose the Right Drywall Screw

body.en: Follow this decision framework: Step 1 — Identify substrate (wood or metal studs). Step 2 — Determine drywall thickness (1/2" or 5/8"). Step 3 — Select screw length (penetration should be at least 5/8" into framing). Step 4 — Choose coating based on environment. Step 5 — Verify local building code requirements. For bulk procurement, consider ordering a mixed assortment to cover common applications. For more information, also see our guides on [self-drilling screws](/en/industry/self-drilling-screws-selection-guide), [coating options](/en/industry/zinc-plated-vs-black-phosphate-screws), [quality standards](/en/industry/fastener-quality-control-iso9001).

q.en: Can I use drywall screws for wood-to-wood connections?

a.en: While possible, drywall screws are not designed for structural wood-to-wood connections. They lack the shear strength of wood screws. Use appropriate wood screws for structural applications.

q.en: What is the difference between S-type and W-type drywall screws?

a.en: S-type has fine threads for metal studs; W-type has coarse threads for wood studs. Using the wrong type can result in poor holding power or stripping.

q.en: How many drywall screws do I need per sheet?

a.en: Standard practice is 32 screws per 4×8 sheet on walls (screws every 12" along studs) and 36 screws per sheet on ceilings (screws every 12" along joists, plus perimeter).
Yes, when used per manufacturer specifications and local building codes. Always verify with your local authority having jurisdiction (AHJ).
